dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Mischief, the four-year-old miniature schnauzer, was featured on the first "The Doc Is In" segment on television, after having his leg amputated at the Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine. "The Doc Is In" was a segment of the Public Broadcasting Service program, "Cure and Caring," that featured the College of Veterinary Medicine. It first aired during the latter's second season in 1999 and was hosted by Dr. Dawn Boothe. This segment featured actual medical cases and discussed how the animals were treated. The show was broadcast from the studios of KAMU at Texas A&M and was produced by TeleVentures Productions.

dcterms_abstract: Ellie Greenbaum with unnamed woman and pets. The Stevenson Companion Animal Life-Care Center provides the physical, emotional, and medical needs of companion animals whose owners are no longer able to provide that care. Clients of the center include pet owners who want to assure their pet's future prior to entering a retirement home, being hospitalized for an extended period, or predeceasing a pet.

dc_contributor: Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences
dc_subject: College administrators--Retirement|Veterinary teachers--Retirement|Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine--Faculty|Playter, Robert|Deans (Education)--Retirement|Retirement|Ophthalmologists--Retirement|Playter, Robert--Anniversaries, etc.|Williams, David A.|Special Events
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Playter and Dr. David A. Williams shake hands while others applaud. Dr. Adams is seen seated in left frame of picture. David Williams founded the GI Lab in 1985 at the University of Florida, when he introduced the assay of serum trypsin-like immunoreactivity (TLI) in the dog to the United States. Over the next two decades students and staff working in the GI Laboratories at Kansas State, Purdue, and most recently Texas A&M Universities established several other novel tests for gastrointestinal diseases for use by veterinarians internationally. His research has been focused on the development and application of new tests for gastrointestinal diseases, particularly those affecting the pancreas, small intestine, stomach and liver of dogs and cats. Dr. Playter joined the faculty in 1970 as an assistant professor in the small animal clinic after earning his master's degree in veterinary medicine and surgery at Texas A&M. Later becoming a full professor and then department head of Small Animal Medicine and Surgery. He left College Station to become the director of the Veterinary Health Center at Cornell University, Ithaca, N.Y. from 1985-1990. Dr. Playter returned to TAMU, College of Veterinary Medicine as the associate dean for Clinical & Outreach Programs, hospital director and director of the continuing education program from 1990-1996. He then resumed teaching, but in 1997 was asked to serve as the interim dean for the college. From 1998 to 1999 he was appointed Executive Associate Dean and then retired in June of 1999.

dc_subject: Dogs on television|Amputees|Dogs|Mischief (Dog)|Miniature schnauzer
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Mischief, the four-year-old miniature schnauzer, was featured on the first "The Doc Is In" segment on television, after having his leg amputated at the Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine. "The Doc Is In" was a segment of the Public Broadcasting Service program, "Cure and Caring," that featured the College of Veterinary Medicine. It first aired during the latter's second season in 1999 and was hosted by Dr. Dawn Boothe. This segment featured actual medical cases and discussed how the animals were treated. The show was broadcast from the studios of KAMU at Texas A&M and was produced by TeleVentures Productions.

dc_subject: Russell, Leon H.|Veterinary laboratories|Veterinary teachers|Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine--Faculty
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Leon H. Russell writing in a notebook. As of February 2017, Dr. Russell is listed as a Senior Professor in the department of Veterinary Integrative Biosciences. Dr. received a B.S. in animal science at University of Missouri in 1953, and his D.V.M. in 1956 also from University of Missouri. His M.P.H. in epidemiology was earned at Tulane University in 1958. Then in 1965 he received a Ph.D. in microbiology at Texas A&M University. His scholarly interests include; epidemiology, medical mycology, zoonotic diseases (rabies), and food toxicology.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Herman F. Dieterich, TAMU '50, holds a sedated lynx kitten. Dr. Herman F. Dieterich was a graduate of Texas A&M University in 1950 and supplemented his education in 1952 at the University of Texas. He was a clinical assistant professor in pathology at the University of Texas in 1980. He retired from private practice in the Dallas area on May 1, 1986 and with his wife Susan moved to southern Colorado. In 1989 Herman and Susan opened the Frisco Creek Wildlife Rehabilitation Center. Dr. Dieterich died April 28, 2003.

dcterms_abstract: Mark Francis standing on a curbside outside a building. Part of his signature appears in the lower left corner of the image. Dr. Mark Francis, first came to Texas A&M in 1888 as the Experiment Station Veterinarian. He served as the first Dean of the School of Veterinary Medicine from 1916 to 1936 and founded the Texas Veterinary Medical Association in 1903.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Professional photograph of Dr. Fred Maurer standing at a bookshelf holding an open book. After his retirement from the U.S. Army, Dr. Maurer became Associate Dean and Distinguished Professor of Pathology at the College of Veterinary Medicine, Texas A&M University, serving in this capacity until September 1974 when he retired. Dr. Maurer also served as the Director of the Institute for Tropical Medicine, of which he was founder, and was instrumental in development of graduate education within the College of Veterinary Medicine.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Portrait of Fred P. Jaggi, Fr. Dr. Jaggi earned his DVM in 1926 from Texas A&M University. From 1926 until 1937 he was in private practice at which time he accepted an appointment to head the newly established Department of Veterinary Hygiene. In 1957, he became head of the newly created Department of Veterinary Public Health. Dr. Jaggi retired in 1965 after serving twenty-eight years with the College of Veterinary Medicine.

dc_subject: Deans (Education)|Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas. School of Veterinary Medicine--Faculty|Price, Alvin Audis|Veterinary teachers
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Alvin Audis Price was Dean between 1957-1973. Dr. Alvin Audis Price was dean of the College of Veterinary Medicine at Texas A&M University from 1957-1973. Dr. Price began his faculty career at Texas A&M in 1949 and went on to head the Department of Veterinary Anatomy. In 1973, after 16 years as dean, Dr. Price resumed his teaching career as a professor in the Department of Veterinary Physiology and Pharmacology. His continued participation in the undergraduate biomedical sciences program led to his appointment as its first director, in 1975. Dr. Price retired in 1988 and died March 11, 2001.

dc_contributor: Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences
dc_subject: Adams, Richard H.|Veterinary teachers|Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine--Faculty|Deans (Education)
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Richard Adams in a dark colored suit. After serving in the Texas A&M Corps of Cadets for four years and graduating with his DVM in 1966, Dr. Adams served as a research veterinarian and Captain in the United States Army at Fort Detrick, MD, for two years. He then received his PhD in pharmacology in 1972, from the University of Pittsburgh while also serving as research associate. In 1972, he became an assistant professor in the department of pharmacology at the University of Texas Southwestern Medical School in Dallas. In 1975, he was tenured and promoted to associate professor. In 1984, he moved to the University of Missouri-Columbia College of Veterinary Medicine where he become a professor and the chairman of the department of veterinary biomedical sciences.

dcterms_abstract: Ronald Martens, Department of Veterinary Large Animal Medicine. Dr. Ronald Martens received his D.V.M. degree at Michigan State University in 1965. His scholarly interests are in equine infectious diseases-pathogenesis, diagnosis and prevention and in particular Rhodococcus equi foal pneumonia. Dr. Martens as of 2017 is professor emeritus and held positions in the department of Large Animal Medicine.

dc_subject: Dogs on television|Amputees|Dogs|Mischief (Dog)|Miniature schnauzer
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Mischief, the four-year-old miniature schnauzer, was featured on the first "The Doc Is In" segment on television, after having his leg amputated at the Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine. "The Doc Is In" was a segment of the Public Broadcasting Service program, "Cure and Caring," that featured the College of Veterinary Medicine. It first aired during the latter's second season in 1999 and was hosted by Dr. Dawn Boothe. This segment featured actual medical cases and discussed how the animals were treated. The show was broadcast from the studios of KAMU at Texas A&M and was produced by TeleVentures Productions.

dc_subject: Dogs on television|Amputees|Dogs|Mischief (Dog)|Miniature schnauzer
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Mischief, the four-year-old miniature schnauzer, was featured on the first "The Doc Is In" segment on television, after having his leg amputated at the Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine. "The Doc Is In" was a segment of the Public Broadcasting Service program, "Cure and Caring," that featured the College of Veterinary Medicine. It first aired during the latter's second season in 1999 and was hosted by Dr. Dawn Boothe. This segment featured actual medical cases and discussed how the animals were treated. The show was broadcast from the studios of KAMU at Texas A&M and was produced by TeleVentures Productions.

dc_subject: Special Events|College administrators--Retirement|Veterinary teachers--Retirement|Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine--Employees|Retirement|Deans (Education)--Retirement|Ophthalmologists--Retirement|Playter, Robert--Anniversaries, etc.
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: An unknown woman stands at a banquet table with "Dr. Playter" place marker while people in room applaud. Dr. Playter (not pictured) joined the faculty in 1970 as an assistant professor in the small animal clinic after earning his master's degree in veterinary medicine and surgery at Texas A&M. Later becoming a full professor and then department head of Small Animal Medicine and Surgery. He left College Station to become the director of the Veterinary Health Center at Cornell University, Ithaca, N.Y. from 1985-1990. Dr. Playter returned to TAMU, College of Veterinary Medicine as the associate dean for Clinical & Outreach Programs, hospital director and director of the continuing education program from 1990-1996. He then resumed teaching, but in 1997 was asked to serve as the interim dean for the college. From 1998 to 1999 he was appointed Executive Associate Dean and then retired in June of 1999.

dc_subject: Dogs on television|Amputees|Dogs|Mischief (Dog)|Miniature schnauzer
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Mischief, the four-year-old miniature schnauzer, was featured on the first "The Doc Is In" segment on television, after having his leg amputated at the Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine. "The Doc Is In" was a segment of the Public Broadcasting Service program, "Cure and Caring," that featured the College of Veterinary Medicine. It first aired during the latter's second season in 1999 and was hosted by Dr. Dawn Boothe. This segment featured actual medical cases and discussed how the animals were treated. The show was broadcast from the studios of KAMU at Texas A&M and was produced by TeleVentures Productions.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Harry Miller III holds a wild cat. At the 1997 Texas Veterinary Medical Association annual meeting, Dr. Harry Miller III was named 1997 Exotic Animal Practitioner of the Year. Miller graduated from the College of Veterinary Medicine in 1970 and has been an adjunct professor at Texas A&M since 1995. Dr. Miller left Texas A&M sometime in 1997 to open "Westgate Pet & Bird Hospital" in Austin, Texas. He retired around 2006, but is often called in for difficult cases.

dc_subject: Dogs on television|Amputees|Dogs|Mischief (Dog)|Miniature schnauzer
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Mischief, the four-year-old miniature schnauzer, was featured on the first "The Doc Is In" segment on television, after having his leg amputated at the Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine. "The Doc Is In" was a segment of the Public Broadcasting Service program, "Cure and Caring," that featured the College of Veterinary Medicine. It first aired during the latter's second season in 1999 and was hosted by Dr. Dawn Boothe. This segment featured actual medical cases and discussed how the animals were treated. The show was broadcast from the studios of KAMU at Texas A&M and was produced by TeleVentures Productions.
